content:
More voices is not more velocity. We proved it with a 58-minute SEV2.

6 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I didn’t know thirty-one people could fit in one Zoom without a decision escaping. I learned Tuesday 1:14 PM when auth service error rate hit 12%.
Incident bridge link posted to #engineering (4,200 members), #incident-bridge, and #random because someone panicked.
1:16–11 people in Zoom.
1:22–19 people.
1:29–31 people. Mix of engineers, PMs, sales, two executives, one intern.
Incident Commander never assigned. Nobody muted. Three simultaneous screen shares.
2:12 PM — rollback approved. 58 minutes after SEV2. Rollback execution: 4 minutes.
Three roles we didn’t assign
The war room playbook is not decoration.
Incident Commander (IC): one voice runs meeting, owns timeline, approves speaking order.
Tech Lead: diagnosis, proposes actions, silent unless adding facts.
Comms Lead: external updates, not in technical debate.
Tuesday:
→ Zero IC — everyone asked “what if we” → Four tech leads — conflicting theories → Comms lead in Zoom but couldn’t get a word in for 22 minutes
# Zoom participant export — 1:29 PM snapshot
# 31 participants, 27 unmuted at least once, 0 agenda doc linkedParalysis dressed as collaboration.
The minute-by-minute protocol we ignored
Minute 0–5 — Assess:
→ IC assigned (senior on-call, not loudest joiner) → Tech lead assigned → Comms lead assigned → Everyone else: listen-only or leave
Minute 5–10 — Communicate:
→ First status template posted (internal) → Zoom chat for links only, not debate
Minute 10–20 — Triage:
→ Tech lead presents one theory with evidence → IC calls for agree/disagree, not new theories
Minute 20+ — Decide:
→ Rollback / fix / escalate — IC calls vote among tech leads only, 3 max
We ran zero of this. We ran 31 people asking if it could be DNS.
→ Production Incident War Room — minute-by-minute first response, three roles, 10-minute status template. This is the doc we had open in another tab while Zoom melted down.
What the extra twenty voices cost
1:14 — SEV2, auth errors 12%
1:18 — tech lead (unofficial) identified bad JWT signing key rotation deploy
1:18–2:08 — debate: rollback vs forward fix vs “wait for key propagation”
→ Sales: “will rollback affect enterprise SSO?” → PM: “can we partial rollback?” → Executive: “what’s the customer count?” → Engineer #14: “maybe it’s not the deploy”
2:08 — IC finally named (by VP, not by process)
2:12 — rollback approved
2:16 — errors 0.3%
50 minutes of debate. 4 minutes of rollback. Signing key deploy was wrong. Rollback was right at 1:18.
→ 30 Production Incidents That Cost $10K+ — war room paralysis case files read the same: fix known early, decision late, headcount high.
The lobby rule we enforce now
Zoom settings:
→ Waiting room enabled for incident bridges → IC admits: tech leads, comms, required escalations only → Everyone else: #incident-bridge Slack for read-only updates
Slack bridge channel:
→ IC posts decisions only → “Questions to tech lead” thread — not live debate → @here banned in incident channels — IC uses @channel max once per 15 min
1:14 Tuesday with lobby rule: estimated 14 people in Zoom, 19 in Slack read-only. Same information. Fewer simultaneous theories.
What NOT to say — Tuesday edition
Heard in the wild:
→ “I think this might be related to the thing last month” (IC: parking lot) → “Can everyone mute” (said 4 times, ignored 4 times) → “Let’s wait 10 more minutes to see” (with 12% errors) → “Who approved this deploy?” (blame — postmortem only)
Allowed:
→ “Evidence shows X” (tech lead) → “Decision: rollback” (IC) → “Next update in 10 minutes” (comms)
Speaking queue — low tech, high leverage
IC maintains numbered queue in Zoom chat:
SPEAKING QUEUE:
1. @tech-lead — deploy correlation evidence
2. @dba — connection count (done)
3. (open)Join queue to talk. Interrupt = IC mutes. Childish? Worked Thursday drill: decision in 19 minutes vs Tuesday’s 58.
The intern question
Intern stayed muted entire call. Good instinct.
Asked in Slack at 1:45: “is there a doc for this?”
Someone pasted war room playbook link. Nobody had opened it.
Process debt is invisible until 31 people need one decision.
Post-Tuesday enforcement
→ Incident bridge link posts to #incident-bridge only — not #engineering → IC training quarterly — 90 minutes, includes lobby drill → Tech lead max 3 — named at minute 5 or IC assigns → Rollback decision SLA: 30 minutes after SEV2 if fix unclear — default rollback when deploy correlation > 80% confidence
58 minutes auth errors. ~8,400 failed logins. $0 direct revenue loss. Massive trust erosion on auth — harder to metric.
I didn’t know I needed permission to leave a crowded Zoom. IC now tells non-essential joiners: “drop, we’ll update Slack.”
Thirty-one people didn’t make us safer. Three roles would have.
The lobby drill that changed behavior
Thursday synthetic SEV2 — no real outage. IC trained on live Zoom.
Participants invited: 35. Admitted to Zoom: 8 (IC, 3 tech, comms, VP observer, scribe). 27 in Slack read-only.
Decision: rollback vs hold — 14 minutes to documented decision. Same IC. Same playbook. Proof the process fits real incidents.
Tuesday had no drill that quarter. Thursday had one 6 days later.
Rollback SLA — default when confidence is high
New rule: deploy correlation confidence > 80% + customer impact SEV2 → rollback decision in 30 minutes max unless tech lead presents counter-evidence with graphs.
Tuesday JWT key deploy: confidence 90% at 1:18. Rollback at 2:12 violated SLA by 24 minutes. Would have auto-triggered escalation to VP at 1:48 under new rule.
Process isn’t bureaucracy when the alternative is 31 people in mute chaos.
The intern who saved the playbook link
Intern muted entire call. Good.
Asked in Slack 1:45: “is there a doc for this?”
Someone pasted war room playbook. Nobody had opened it.
Intern unmuted at 1:52: “IC is supposed to assign roles at minute 5?”
Awkward silence. VP: “Let’s do that now.”
IC assigned 1:53. Rollback approved 2:12. Nineteen minutes of role clarity after thirty-nine minutes of chaos.
Lesson: junior engineers read docs when seniors panic-post bridge links to #engineering.
Audio hygiene — the mute problem
27 unmuted at peak. Background: dog, keyboard, someone eating.
IC now has co-host mute power. First use Tuesday 1:31: controversial. Second use Thursday drill: normal.
Zoom setting: “Mute participants upon entry” default for incident bridges. Unmute to speak via queue only.
58 minutes auth errors included ~4 minutes lost to “can everyone mute” repetition. Small. Insulting. Fixable.
Auth error customer impact — the number we undercounted
~8,400 failed logins Tuesday. Direct revenue: $0 — no checkout blocked.
Indirect:
→ 340 password reset requests spike (users thought they forgot password) → 2 enterprise customers opened reliability tickets → Security team asked if breach — JWT key rotation coincidence looked like compromise
War room noise delayed comms template: “We are investigating auth errors.” Published 2:04 PM. Should have been 1:20 PM.
Process failure compounded technical failure.
Tuesday postmortem — the headcount graph
Postmortem included Zoom join/leave timeline overlaid with decision points.
1:18 — fix known. 31 people in room. 0 decisions. 1:53 — IC assigned. 28 people. 1 decision pending. 2:12 — rollback approved. 24 people (some left).
Correlation: decisions happen after IC, not after headcount.
We show this graph in IC training now. Embarrassing. Effective.
Lobby rule pushback — and why we kept it
Sales argued they need live Zoom for “customer context.” Agreed — sales listens in Slack #incident-readonly, not Zoom.
PM argued they need to ask scope questions. Agreed — PM posts in Slack thread, IC triages to tech lead if technical.
31 people in Zoom was not collaboration. It was anxiety seeking company.
8 people in Zoom with 23 in Slack was Tuesday’s Thursday drill. 14-minute decision. Proof.
IC assignment at minute 5 — non-negotiable now
PagerDuty incident template field: inc_assigned: yes/no. Cannot mark mitigated without IC name.
Tuesday: IC assigned 1:53–39 minutes late. Thursday drill: IC assigned 1:19–5 minutes.
Same people. Template enforcement. 34 minutes saved on auth alone if Tuesday had the field.

Froquiz’s Senior Dev Challenge includes war room scenarios — practice assigning IC before the thirty-second joiner asks if it’s DNS. → Froquiz
